title: Why Meta is the primary growth channel for apps
insight: Meta has 4 billion active users across its family of apps, making it the most scalable single ad channel for mobile apps. It offers a wide range of placements you can preview live, multiple campaign strategies (web-to-app, direct install, lead gen), and the ability to research competitor creatives via the Ad Library. If you want one channel to master, Meta is the best starting point.
raw_excerpt: It has 4 billion active users across meta family of apps so there's a lot of people here it's very very scalable... you can really bring together a whole bunch of different strategies here you can go web to app you can do direct to the App Store install campaigns.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel

title: Start with Meta SDK and track trial start as the key event
insight: For new advertisers, use the free Meta SDK instead of an MMP to avoid third-party complexity. Track trial start as your primary conversion event — it's the most important signal for subscription apps. Add 3-5 in-app events max (e.g., onboarding complete, activation actions), but don't replicate your full product analytics in the SDK. Only track signals relevant to user acquisition.
raw_excerpt: If you're really new and you're setting this up as your first channel then just use the meta SDK it's for free... start tracking your most important in-app events for most of the subscription apps out there it's really the trial start.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Implement Meta SDK (free, no third-party MMP needed initially)
Track trial start as your primary conversion event
Add 2-4 additional proxy events that happen early in the funnel and correlate with paid conversion
Do NOT replicate full product analytics in the SDK

title: Track early qualification events that correlate with trial-to-paid conversion
insight: Beyond trial start, identify user behaviors in the first 24 hours that correlate with higher trial-to-paid conversion (e.g., completing onboarding, activating a core feature, setting up an account). Feed these as additional conversion events to Meta so the algorithm can make smarter optimization decisions. This is more advanced but valuable for improving signal quality.
raw_excerpt: I would first try to find these user behaviors that lead to higher conversion from trial to paid and then that's something you might want to know on the ad side.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Analyze which early user behaviors (first 24h) correlate with trial-to-paid conversion
Add those as additional SDK events (e.g., onboarding_complete, first_action)
Keep total events to 3-5 max

title: Minimum 10K/month media spend to validate Meta as a channel
insight: You need at least 10K EUR/USD per month in media spend to properly test Meta. The algorithm needs sufficient data to find the right audience with broad targeting, and you need budget to test multiple creative concepts. On top, allocate ~5K for creative production. Below this threshold, the channel likely won't produce valid learnings. For very small budgets (2-300/month), Apple Search Ads is a better starting point.
raw_excerpt: If you don't have more than 10K to spend purely on media really then it's going to be tough because how you're going to set up your campaigns is mostly going to be broad targeting and the algorithm needs to do all the work.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Budget at least 10K/month for media spend on Meta
Add ~5K for creative production costs
If budget is under 5K/month, start with Apple Search Ads instead
Consider web campaigns (Stripe/pixel) if budget is tight — they can work at lower spend due to better signal quality

title: Default to broad targeting — let creatives do the targeting
insight: For most new advertisers, broad targeting outperforms interest-based targeting. Interest targeting is based on stale browsing behavior (what users liked on Facebook years ago), restricts audience size (raising CPMs), and rarely delivers higher quality. Instead, use creative content to attract your target audience — e.g., a parenting app should use broad targeting with creatives that speak to parents. Meta's algorithm will learn who converts.
raw_excerpt: I wouldn't recommend using interests or anything like that because in the end it's not super efficient... I'd rather go broad and make good creatives that then tailor to the audience that you are looking to attract.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Start with broad targeting (no interest or demographic restrictions)
Use creative messaging to attract your target audience instead
Only use lookalikes if you have 10K+ entries to upload (expect ~20% match rate)
Base lookalikes on subscribers/paying users, not installs

title: Lookalike audiences need 10K entries to reach 2K matched minimum
insight: For effective lookalikes, upload at least 10,000 identifiers (Google Ad IDs, IDFAs, emails). Expect only ~20% match rate since many emails don't match Facebook accounts created years ago. You need to land on ~2,000 matched users for Meta to build a quality lookalike. Base lookalikes on subscribers/paying users, not just installers — paying the premium for a restricted audience is only worth it if you're targeting high-value users.
raw_excerpt: I usually recommend that you want at least 10,000 entries to upload... just to make sure that even if they only match 20% of the people you still land on a 2K audience which is then relevant for them to create a good lookalike.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Gather at least 10K user identifiers (device IDs, emails) before creating lookalikes
Use paying subscribers as the seed audience, not just installers
If you have enough data, segment further (e.g., iOS subscribers, specific markets)
Run lookalike and broad targeting in parallel if lookalikes are available

title: Consolidate campaigns: one campaign, one ad set at low budgets
insight: At low budgets, keep your account structure as consolidated as possible — one campaign, one ad set. Meta recommends 50 conversion events per ad set per week for optimal algorithm performance. Every additional ad set splits your conversion volume. Only add ad sets once you have enough weekly conversions to support them (e.g., 200 trials/week could support 2 ad sets of 100 each).
raw_excerpt: Make it as consolidated as possible rather have one campaign one adset if you're at this only 10K budget because the more you split out adsets the more conversion events you need... they want 50 events per ad set per week.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Start with one campaign, one ad set
Target 50+ conversion events per ad set per week
Only add a second ad set when weekly conversions can support it
Don't obsess over the 50/week number — 10/week is better than 5, it's a sliding scale

title: Separate ad sets by creative type to prevent UGC from cannibalizing other formats
insight: Never test static ads vs video ads in the same ad set. UGC videos always win spend because they deliver on Reels (cheap CPMs, high engagement from young users), starving static ads of budget. But statics deliver on Facebook/Instagram feed to older, higher-converting audiences. A static ad with 50% higher cost-per-trial can still be more profitable if it reaches older users with 50% higher trial-to-paid conversion. Separate creative types into different ad sets to control budget allocation.
raw_excerpt: I would never test a static ad versus a video ad because in the end they are meant for different placements... UGC will always get all the spend because it delivers on Reels which has cheap CPMs young users interacting with it.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Create separate ad sets for static ads and video/UGC ads
Never combine video and static in the same ad set — UGC will cannibalize all spend
Evaluate creative types by down-funnel metrics (trial-to-paid), not just cost-per-trial
Statics on Facebook feed often reach older, higher-value audiences despite higher CPTs

title: Young audiences (18-24) produce cheap trials but poor trial-to-paid conversion
insight: Meta's algorithm optimizes for cheap cost-per-trial, which often means buying young audiences (18-24) who have low purchasing power. These users start trials readily but convert to paid subscriptions at much lower rates. Use onboarding surveys to identify age composition of your cohorts and correlate with trial-to-paid conversion. Consider excluding under-25 audiences in manual campaigns if data shows poor down-funnel performance.
raw_excerpt: What I often see is that advertisers optimize for a very cheap cost per trial which often comes from young audiences because they're cheaper they don't have high purchasing power... you're buying a lot of people 18 to 24 at cheap cost per trial but their trial conversion is going to be bad.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Add an age question to your onboarding survey
Break down trial-to-paid conversion by age cohort in product analytics
Consider excluding age 18-24 in manual campaign ad sets if conversion data supports it
Don't optimize purely for cheap cost-per-trial — evaluate by LTV

title: Use onboarding surveys to bridge the SKAN attribution gap
insight: Since SKAN doesn't provide user-level tracking past trial start, onboarding surveys are critical to understand audience composition. Ask age, gender, and discovery source during onboarding. This lets you break down conversion metrics by demographic cohorts and correlate with media buying data (placement distribution, creative type spend). This is how you determine if SKAN campaigns are actually profitable.
raw_excerpt: These onboarding surveys are just so valuable if you ask users to give you that information then you can break down all your metrics on these different hierarchy levels and that again should inform your media buying.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Add age, gender, and 'how did you hear about us' questions to onboarding
Break down trial-to-paid conversion and renewal rates by survey responses
Use placement distribution data from Meta + survey data to determine true ROI per audience segment
Compare SKAN-reported results with product analytics — expect ~40% under-reporting

title: SKAN under-reports by ~40% — always cross-reference with product analytics
insight: SKAN consistently under-reports conversions by approximately 40% or more compared to actual product analytics data. This is partly because view-through conversions (40-50% share on video placements like Reels/Stories) show up as App Store search, not as Meta-attributed. AEM also under-reports by 10-20%. Always compare SKAN/AEM data against your actual subscription revenue (via RevenueCat, Adapty, etc.) to assess true channel value.
raw_excerpt: I often see around about 40% if not higher discrepancies in what SKAN is reporting and what I end up seeing in my product analytics... even with AEM I often see that still it's like 10-20% less trials coming in than I actually see in my product analytics.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Compare SKAN-reported trials with actual product analytics data
Monitor App Store search trends alongside Meta spend — view-through traffic shows as search
Use blended metrics (incremental revenue vs total ad spend) as your source of truth
Expect SKAN to under-report by ~40% and AEM by ~10-20%

title: AEM vs SKAN: use AEM for creative testing, SKAN may serve better audiences for BAU
insight: AEM (Advanced Event Measurement) provides real-time data without SKAN's 2-3 day postback delay and privacy thresholds (88 installs/day minimum). This makes AEM ideal for creative testing where quick iteration matters. However, SKAN sometimes delivers to better-converting audiences in business-as-usual campaigns. Run both and compare — attribution frameworks aren't either/or decisions. Results vary by account.
raw_excerpt: AEM is cool for creative testing because it means quicker results so sometimes I have my creative tests on AEM while my business as usual campaigns actually run on SKAN because I've seen it serve the better audience.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Use AEM for creative testing campaigns (faster results, no privacy thresholds)
Test SKAN for BAU campaigns — it may deliver better-converting audiences
Run both frameworks and compare performance per account
Don't treat attribution choice as permanent — test and iterate

title: Test creatives on Android first — results port to iOS ~90% of the time
insight: If you have an Android app, run creative tests there first. Results transfer to iOS about 90% of the time, Android tracking has fewer headaches than iOS/SKAN, and CPMs are generally cheaper. Use a separate testing campaign from your business-as-usual to avoid disrupting existing performance. Test on a weekly or bi-weekly cadence.
raw_excerpt: Test on Android because in 90% of the cases the results are going to be able to be ported to iOS and hold up and Android tracking is just a bit less of a headache these days.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Run creative tests on Android first if available
Use a separate campaign for testing — don't mix with BAU
Test new creatives on a weekly or bi-weekly cadence
Consider proxy markets (Brazil, Philippines) for cheaper testing — expect 70-80% result portability

title: 3-5 creatives per campaign — Meta only spends on top 1-2 anyway
insight: Start with 3-5 creatives per campaign. Even in Advantage+ campaigns that allow more, Meta concentrates spend on the top 1-2 performers while the rest get negligible delivery. If you upload 10 creatives, you'll never learn how the bottom 8 performed. Keep the number low, learn what works, then make informed decisions about what to test next.
raw_excerpt: I usually do three to five per campaign... meta always puts spend only on the first one or two and the others don't do anything so if you put in 10 creatives then you're never going to learn how did the bottom eight perform.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Limit to 3-5 creatives per ad set
Use multiple ads (not Dynamic Creative) to get granular per-ad reporting
Analyze results and iterate on winners before adding new creatives

title: Manual campaigns offer more control for beginners — test Advantage+ later
insight: Start with manual campaigns for more control over age exclusions and targeting at the ad set level. Advantage+ requires account-level settings changes for similar restrictions, which is more complex. Both campaign types can buy totally different audiences and placements. Test both over time and analyze age group distribution and placement breakdown to understand what each is buying. Manual gives more visibility early on.
raw_excerpt: I tend to use manual in the beginning because I want a little bit more control... over time I would definitely test both and see how these campaign types react because they can end up buying totally different audiences.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Start with manual campaigns for easier age/placement control
Don't run both manual and ASC simultaneously at low budgets — pick one
Check age and placement breakdowns in Ads Manager regularly
Test Advantage+ once you have baseline performance to compare against

title: Use bid caps to improve efficiency — start at 5x CPA and gradually lower
insight: Adding bid caps signals Meta to spend efficiently rather than exhausting daily budget at any price. Start campaigns on open bids to gather conversion history, then add a bid cap at 5x your current CPA (e.g., $100 cap if CPA is $20). This alone can improve cost-per-trial by 20-30%. Gradually decrease the bid by 10-20% daily until you find the point where spend drops to 80-90% of budget — that's the optimal level. Be prepared for occasional days with zero spend.
raw_excerpt: Adding the bid caps does have the effect that you spend 20-30% maybe less on your cost per trial... start campaigns on open bids, add in bids after gathering conversion history at a very high level like 5x of the current CPA.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Launch campaigns on open bids to build conversion history
After initial data, add bid cap at 5x your observed CPA
Decrease bid cap by 10-20% daily
Find the sweet spot where you're spending 80-90% of daily budget
Accept that some days will under-spend — this is the tradeoff for better efficiency

title: Optimizing for direct purchases can produce smarter algorithm decisions than trial optimization
insight: When Meta optimizes for trial starts, it can't distinguish between high-value and low-value trial users. With direct purchase optimization (no trial), the algorithm buys older, higher-converting traffic because a purchase is an unambiguous value signal. If your trial-to-paid conversion varies significantly by audience segment, consider whether removing the trial or excluding low-value SKUs from the conversion event improves algorithm targeting quality.
raw_excerpt: I have this one client who's running on actually direct purchases he doesn't have a trial at all and I see the algorithm making much smarter decisions... I'm buying much older traffic because those are the people that would end up converting in the trial as well but if I optimize for the trial meta doesn't know.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Analyze LTV differences between trial and direct purchase users
Consider removing deeply-discounted SKUs from the conversion event sent to Meta
Test a campaign optimizing for direct purchase (no trial) if your product supports it
At low volume, send all purchase types as one event to maximize signal

title: Create all creatives in 9:16 and let Meta auto-crop for other placements
insight: Design all creatives in 9:16 (vertical) format with key elements centered in safe zones. Meta will auto-crop to 4:5 for video and 1:1 for images. This simplifies production significantly — one creative per concept instead of multiple sizes. Ensure text and important visuals stay within the center crop area.
raw_excerpt: I do everything in 9 to 16 these days and then meta auto-crops them for me to 4 to 5 on video and to 1 to 1 on image... it just makes it a lot quicker for me if I only have one creative to upload.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Design all creatives in 9:16 format
Keep key elements centered within 4:5 and 1:1 safe zones
Upload one version and let Meta handle cropping per placement

title: Only promote winning creatives to BAU when performance justifies disrupting learning phase
insight: Moving a winning creative from your testing campaign to business-as-usual triggers a new learning phase, which temporarily hurts performance. Only do it if the winner is dramatically better (will catapult performance) or if your BAU campaign is already declining. Keep moderate winners in a backlog for when performance dips. Don't disrupt a well-performing BAU campaign for marginal gains.
raw_excerpt: Only if I find a winner which is so good that I know this is going to catapult my performance... or I find a winner which is doing 5-10% better, that's one where I would keep it for later when my main campaign is kind of struggling.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Maintain a backlog of proven-but-not-yet-deployed creative winners
Only push to BAU if the creative is dramatically better or BAU is declining
Accept learning phase disruption only when the expected gain justifies it

title: Creative fatigue management: iterate, pause, and reintroduce
insight: All creatives degrade over time as they exhaust their optimal audience. Extend creative lifespan by iterating — change small elements like flipping layout, swapping backgrounds, or adjusting text while keeping the core concept. When a creative is fully fatigued, pause it for a few months then reintroduce it — this often restores performance. Always have new creatives in production so you're prepared when current winners decline.
raw_excerpt: The level of performance is always going to degrade over time... iterating means you change small parts of it... consider pausing it for a while and then you can retry in a few months and that often times helps you bring them back at better performance.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Iterate on winning creatives — change small elements (flip layout, swap colors, adjust text)
Pause fully fatigued creatives and reintroduce after a few months
Maintain continuous creative production as a percentage of total spend (10-30%)
Don't wait for performance to crash before producing new creatives

title: Creative testing budget should be 10-50% of total spend depending on account maturity
insight: When starting out, creative testing may be 50-100% of your spend since everything is new. As you scale, aim for 10-30% of total spend on testing. Track creative production budget separately as a percentage of media spend — this ensures continuous creative development. The BAU campaign's performance should subsidize testing costs. Never let production pipeline go dormant during good performance periods.
raw_excerpt: In the beginning it might be still 50% of my budget on testing because the account is fresh... if you're at scale at 100K or something a month then your testing budget should be in the realms of like 10 to 20 maybe 30%.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: New accounts: allocate up to 50% of spend on creative testing
At scale (100K+/month): aim for 10-30% testing budget
Track creative production costs as a separate budget line, scaled to media spend
Keep producing creatives during good periods to prepare for performance dips

title: Don't optimize for link clicks or installs — go as deep in the funnel as possible
insight: Optimizing for upper-funnel events (link clicks, app installs) buys the lowest quality traffic — people who click a lot or cheap leftover inventory nobody else is bidding on. Always optimize for the deepest meaningful conversion event you can (trial start, purchase). The further up the funnel your optimization event, the lower quality users you'll attract.
raw_excerpt: The further you move up the funnel the lower quality traffic you're going to be buying if you buy link clicks you're going to get people that click a lot on links but they're not going to install and they're definitely not gonna purchase.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Never optimize for link clicks or installs
Optimize for trial start or purchase as your conversion event
Don't use app install campaigns for creative testing — results won't be comparable to AEO campaigns

title: Web-to-app campaigns work at lower budgets due to better signal quality
insight: Web campaigns that convert via landing page + Stripe bypass SKAN entirely, sending conversion data through the pixel and Conversion API. This dramatically improves signal quality, allowing Meta to optimize faster and more accurately. This can work at lower budgets than traditional app install campaigns. Growing number of subscription apps are adopting web-to-app funnels for this reason.
raw_excerpt: If you have a web campaign for example you're converting people on the landing page via Stripe... the signal quality is a lot higher you're sending back through the pixel and conversion API tracking... I've seen people starting to spend on lower budgets that way.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Consider a web-to-app funnel if budget is below 10K/month
Set up landing page with Stripe + Meta pixel + Conversion API
Benefit: bypass SKAN limitations, get real-time full-funnel data
Test web funnel conversion rates against direct app install campaigns

title: Mirror winning ad creatives in App Store screenshots for consistency
insight: Use your best-performing ad creative (or its messaging/design) as one of your App Store screenshots. This creates visual continuity between the ad and the store page, increasing conversion rates. Users who saw your ad on Meta will recognize the app in search results, even if a competitor ranks higher for your brand keyword. Optimize the default product page rather than custom product pages if you're a small-to-mid developer with Meta as your primary channel.
raw_excerpt: I would even have one of my ad designs as one of my screenshots... you kind of have that one-to-one link in what's happening on the ad side what's happening on the store... they will still recognize that the second one is you.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Add your best-performing ad creative or its messaging as an App Store screenshot
Optimize the default product page (skip CPPs if you're small/mid-size)
Test app icon variations for click-through rate improvement
Focus on rating volume for ASO — it has the best correlation with search/browse traffic

title: Master one channel before diversifying — go deep before going broad
insight: Spending 20K across five channels without mastering any produces worse results than going deep on one. Master Meta (or your chosen channel) first, then add complementary channels. Apple Search Ads is a natural complement for brand protection once you scale. TikTok shares creative overlap with Meta, making it an efficient second channel. Only diversify once your primary channel is consistently profitable.
raw_excerpt: I would always recommend like master one channel first like decide on one it doesn't have to be meta but you want to go deep before going broad because spending 20K on five channels without knowing any one of them very good is not going to get you good results.
source_title: App Masters YouTube - Meta Ads Unlocked: How to Make Facebook Your Primary Growth Channel
actionable_steps: Pick one primary channel and master it before adding others
Add Apple Search Ads for brand protection once you scale on Meta
Consider TikTok as a second paid channel — creative overlap with Meta reduces production costs
Only diversify once your primary channel is consistently profitable
